speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3 of the interview with Eva Neumann, June 4, 1996. You'd been describing earlier the routines of life that went on when you were growing up. How-- did this-- did these things change under the Hungarians, the observation of holidays or-- or the various food preparation times during the year, things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everything become like underground. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You weren't allowed to show that if you did have something. And I remember hurrying the prayers in shul and getting home quickly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't-- compared it wasn't that bad. At the time, it felt bad. But it was a lot doing in this town. It became a big busy town. And everybody in town was busy with the soldiers, with coming and people coming from out of town to find people, because a lot of them got shot on the way in or died or perished. And they took-- you know, like we were the center in the middle of the town kind of. And they used to come and ask and find and look for these people. But I still remember that they were allowed, for instance, those few very Orthodox soldiers, so-called-- they weren't soldiers. They weren't an army. They were working--

speaker: interviewer
text: Laborers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laborers in the army. It's called the Jewish army. We were still allowed to take to the barracks kosher food, which I think looking back on it was wonderful. They did allow that, so it wasn't so 100%. And there were-- I remember we had about 30, 40 soldiers for seder one-- I think it was the last seder. So it was-- you know, it was quite-- quite something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- for us children, it was wonderful. It was all these handsome-- they still wore uniform, most of them. The-- the-- I can't say the food was very wonderful. It was potatoes on potatoes on potatoes, this different color, different shape, and different size. We managed a bit of chicken soup. We managed a matzah, which was all homemade, kind of. And there was enough food. You know, we managed. And that was very-- you know, first the time spent, Jews have got this nature, make the atmosphere, especially on a holiday, on a yontif, everybody tries their hardest to make it jolly and exciting and grateful. You know, you come to a point when you're just grateful even if you just manage to have the four courses of wine to drink. It's-- it's-- was a very big event. They came back from wherever they were and they still managed to have a seder. It didn't mean that much after the war. But at that time, it still meant a lot, because all those old boys were still from religious and they tried. And so to them, it was a very, very big thing to be able to kind of have kosher and celebrate together, my father sitting in the head of the table. And you know, everybody knew what was going on. You didn't have to explain. Everybody joined in. And it was-- it was beautiful, touching, but very sad. Everybody was very jolly, but looking back on it, too jolly. [LAUGHS] You know, they were trying very hard to-- to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about everything that was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I can't remember me being-- everything was exciting. You see, when you live in a small town, nothing really exciting happens. You don't go to pictures. You don't have television, you know? You know, so whatever happened, it was exciting. But there was a fear. There was an underlining fear, which you weren't so conscious of, but you were uncomfortable, because your parents were sad. There nothing to do. You weren't allowed to take a job or see to your land or-- you know, everything was very tense and frightening. And you know, I keep on repeating these things. When you're a child and you've got your parents, nothing is frightening. You-- you've got your protection. You don't think of fear as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can't tell you what I felt then. We felt an atmosphere, but what the atmosphere was I can't tell you. I can't tell-- I can't-- I was too young to know that tomorrow it's not going to be good. And they were frightened of that, whereas I-- they protected us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say, you know, what could happen and maybe what we heard may be true. We never-- you know, they wouldn't have told us that. I wouldn't tell my children either. It's not-- so we were protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your time spent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we did have lessons. My father was very strict about that. He sat with us for hours. That's the only thing he could do, learn with a boy and-- boys, and gave us homework and did sums with us. And then we played and, you know, like what children usually do. I did a lot of embroidery and knitting. And you couldn't buy wool, so what you knitted yesterday, you unknit today and you re-knitted it. And it was fun, you know. We used to go up to attic to find old jumpers which we could undo to get the wool, and-- you know, it was-- this is the kind of things we did as girls. And the boys were-- they had a few hours playing, playing ball, football. I don't know. There wasn't very much to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so in 1942, your father was called away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was called away, which was-- my mother couldn't cope. She was very, very anxious. She was always-- she was the little-- little lady was always protected. So we children like had to protect her. And she was-- I can't explain it. You know, you talk about your mother and everybody would say the same about their mother. She was very-- she was a bit more educated than the town-- townfolk there. And she was very loving and very supportive. And so we kind of children felt we have got to support her. But she took it very, very, very badly that my father wasn't there. And my father had a very tough time. It didn't last very long, but it was enough to squash your self-confidence and realize that things aren't as good as you hoped it will be. He had a terrific sense of humor and he managed with his sense of humor somehow to survive. But he didn't come back the big strong man who left. He lost a lot of weight and he shrunk a bit I think. He must have gone through hell there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was working in the-- in a-- [SIGHS] food, where They collected all the food for the army. Everybody who grew something went to this-- it was called a form. I don't know. It's probably some shortening for that. And he was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Sorry. He was-- he was put in there and they had to carry the sacks into wagons, which went to the front, to the army. And it was physical and mental. It was terrible. I don't even think they were so short of food, as short of-- they stripped them of anything human. These Gypsies reversed their position and they become the bosses over these gentleman, because it's only the few. And they really made them do things which wasn't quite human. And physically, these people always sat in a chair and wrote and read and learned. And all of a sudden, they were put into this very, very hard physical work and sleeping on the floor. And you know, it-- it was very tough. He came back already as not the same person who left. I felt that. I didn't realize, but-- I thought he wasn't well. Probably wasn't well, but not like didn't have pneumonia or whooping cough or anything. He just--

speaker: interviewer
text: His spirit was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I think that's when they started to worry and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said he-- he used his sense of humor, what do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was very-- he-- see, his aim was always to help others, so he used his sense of humor always to-- they were telling us stories for, you know-- used to pretend that the potato was a chicken and the chicken potato. You know, he kept them a little bit amused to be able to survive. And out of I think that group one man survived who told me the story. I met him after the reparation and was telling me--

speaker: interviewer
text: What group was that? Out of which group?

speaker: interviewee
text: This group who--

speaker: interviewer
text: The group that was taken away to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It was unique group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used his sense of humor when he was away with them to help himself and others to get through.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, when you-- when you say you had to help out your mother and your father was away and becoming weaker in a way, did you feel any special responsibilities as the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I always felt a special responsibility, because I was told that I was-- you know, I'm the oldest and I've got to give a good example and I was-- had to be a very good girl because I was the oldest. So this role came on me and I just took over. I mean, there was nothing to take over. There was no babies to change. You know, tried to kind of say don't get up in the morning and call the doctor and, you know, just kind of look after her, exactly as my father would have done. You follow their example.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13, 14, 14. When I left, I was just 15, so this was a year before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was 13 or 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it to-- to live your life protected in a small town and all of a sudden to have to assume such responsibilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't feel it. You're not conscious of it. You don't realize sometime, you know-- when you start taking responsibility, you feel very proud of yourself. You feel, oh, here I'm the big girl taking responsibility. I never felt, you know, that I'm here the special guardian who has got to take responsibilities. It was just a natural-- you know, I used to run to my aunt to sleep there. I didn't run to my aunt to sleep there, because Mommy needed me, you know. It's that kind-- but you're not conscious of it. You don't think-- in any circumstances children aren't conscious that they've got to assume the responsibility. It just falls on you and you take it for granted. And as a child, you're proud of it, you know. You're becoming somebody. So it didn't-- didn't bother me. I didn't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you conscious of the situation becoming more grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. But I don't know why. We don't-- I-- maybe they knew, but I don't know why. I mean, there was less food and less heating. And there was no new clothes. And there was-- you weren't allowed to do this, that, and the other. And it was-- you had to be home at 6 o'clock. And you weren't allowed to listen to the radio. We used to huddle in a corner and listen to the radio. And Father always used to interpret. Churchill said it's going to be OK, [LAUGHS] you know. But things were hard. We were left one room to sleep in, all of us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the last space, the atmosphere was a lot of people, but it was very, very very tense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living in the same house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had only one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were allocated one room to sleep in, because the soldiers needed it. But it was the big dining room, so we cleared it up in the morning. And the Jewish soldiers came and we had a meal. And it was-- you know, we tried to do as much as possible. We still had the potatoes and whatever needed to feed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other special regulations instituted against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Just one word, no Jews. You are not allowed this, that, or anything. You weren't allowed to do anything. You weren't allowed to trade or sell or buy or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you weren't specific-- you know, there wasn't so much during that that was specifically told to you what not to do. You were just not allowed to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we tried a bit of protect here and you knew the-- the governor, so he allowed-- you know. You know how you try to-- everybody achieved something. So you had to make your deals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deals. Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in that, or was it your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents. But I still remember terrible dreams. And thinking back on it, I think I must have been more conscious of the trouble than I realized, because the dreams I still remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always remember that my parents are going to be killed and I'm going to be separated from my parents. And I mean, these were dreams, but probably there was such atmosphere that made me dream. You know, the nightmares were there, but didn't know specifically why they were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think maybe the words of the-- of the Polish girl stayed with you, even though your father tried to discount them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's possible. Possible. But you know, I would need a psychiatrist to-- to work it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sort that all out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I would-- [LAUGHS] not important. That became so unimportant in the future events that it didn't matter. Those were still good times, as far as I was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, it was the day after Pesach. It was-- Pesach finished on Saturday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the Pesach where you'd had all of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all of the Jewish laborers with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. In the morning, my father went to the-- to shul with the boys and they turned back. Said, what's happening? No Jews are allowed on this street. And there was a Hungarian general, somebody, living in one of these rooms in our house. And my father called him in and asked him what's happening. He said he doesn't know. So he said, but try to hide your jewelry and-- he must have known.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we gave him some of our jewelry. And then we went down to the cellar and my father called me I should go with, that I should know where he hid whatever we had, you know, the-- the diamond ring. I mean, not saying we had that much. But to them it was whatever they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and about 11, 12 o'clock, they just-- at every door of the house simultaneously, they came in, the Germans, the SS or whatever they were. And they just said, take all your jewelry off and pack a case, just enough for you to carry. And that was the first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's how it started. And we were all taken to the shul hall. They collected us there. We stayed there overnight. And next day, we went to Munkacs, to the ghetto, stayed there for about three weeks with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad. Bad. But you see, bad compared to what I left, not so bad for what came after. It was very bad. It-- you know, they allocated us like six feet by six feet for each family. And everybody had to go out to work. I don't even know what they did. I know my father and my mother cooked in the main kitchen. And it's interesting. I was at home. I was left in this cubicle, whatever was allocated to us. And an SS came by. And he said, come on. You're going to clean the toilets. I was 15 and I never cleaned a toilet before. And I was quite proud that he thought I was old enough. You know, it's-- and I went. And they told me what to do, a row of toilets to clean. And funnily enough, I did not feel humiliated. I felt I'm part of this, whatever is happening. You see, when you're a child, I don't think parents know when to draw you into the situation. And everybody was out working. Only people from my age downwards, you know, we were out at home doing nothing. At home-- I mean in this little-- we sat outside on the stone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was [? involved, ?] so I was very happy. And when my parents came back from work in the evening and I was, you know, you know what I did today? [LAUGHS] I cleaned the toilets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My mother's face. My little girl, cleaning toilets. [CRYING] So-- and that was the life there for about three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after three weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken from your town to Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, you know, ordinary-- no. Sorry. By lorries. Lorries came and however many could get on to the lorry, we-- we went to Munkacs. And then we stayed there for about three weeks. Things-- they organize certain amount of people. And then I think it went according to alphabetical order, so many went to the next stage, to the wagons. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people being gathered from all over in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All over the towns, all over the surrounding towns. They gathered them in this-- it was a brick factory. And they brought them from all over, the religious and from Munkacs themselves. And when it was full, then transport went to what we know now Auschwitz. And that's how we got into these wagons. And we went, not knowing where to, terrible. Filled the wagon up so badly that you couldn't even sit. And a lot of people died there and smell and hunger and heat and crying and it was-- But it was still-- somehow, however bad it was, you were together, you know. There was-- I missed out a part. When we went in this ghetto, we had a worker, a non-Jew who used to work for us. And he came to the-- to the ghetto. And he wanted to take the children to prevent-- protect them. [DRINKS] It was in the middle of the night. He got through the wire somehow. He got to-- he was very loyal servant. And he wanted to take us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a conference between my parents. And my father said, I'm not letting the boys go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're too young. So it was me. So my father-- so they decided-- I overheard. I mean, it was-- you couldn't hide by then. Everything was talked-- everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's my father. My father and my mother discussed it. Tell you what. Let her decide. If she wants to go, let her go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, no way. I'm-- I'm not going. If we go, go together. And I didn't go. And so many times people asked me, are you sorry you didn't go? Was never sorry I didn't go. First of all, looking back on it now, I wouldn't have survived, because they would have found me somewhere, somehow. It was too small to hide. It wasn't like hiding in a big town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whatever went through, I think if I would have come back and survived hiding with this man and my parents would have gone to Auschwitz, I don't think I would have ever forgiven myself. I-- I think I'd done the right choice, whatever, because to live with that guilt, I don't think I could have lived with it. But there was no question I wasn't going to go without my parents. We had the family together, so whatever happens, together. And then we got to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little more about the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train, it-- this-- it was a death plane-- train. It's all huddled in. And it was quiet and then screaming and then crying and then, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] occasionally they pushed us in with a water and-- it was-- it was-- yeah. It's undescribable. It's-- you must have heard it before when people talk about it. You can't describe the-- you know, you can't describe a smell and an atmosphere and-- and-- and a child who's never seen death or a body lying with a body. You know, it's-- And then when we arrived, they opened the wagons. And-- and you could see these bodies taking from all the-- from all the wagons. And you know, it's-- it's not describable. It's not-- not something you can explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: My thoughts. Yes. That's when the thoughts of hunger came into-- cold and hunger. And you don't know which point in life hunger and self-preservation takes over. And at this point, it was still not bad, because I was still with my parents. So maybe I was hungry and cold, but I wasn't worried, because I had my parents to protect me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wasn't even conscious of that. I'm only conscious of it now. Why wasn't I more panicky? Because I had them. We were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: My little brother panicked. I think he had an intuition. He was crying the whole way through. He was really in a bad way and wouldn't leave my mother and-- he might have had an intuition. I was just protected and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, then once you start being physically uncomfortable and really hungry and really thirsty and really cold, then your thoughts, where will I take my next food? You don't-- you know, it was-- and it was so bad that you didn't-- you become numb. You can't possibly have feelings.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something which-- I don't know, animal-like. I don't think animals could survive it. It's-- you know, can you imagine like sardines squashed together in a little tiny place and smell and-- you know, men chanting and davening and crying. And you know, it's like a-- a mass of something which you can't describe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we arrived and-- very, very, very panicky, because I think by then the Germans were panicky. And they were collecting people in a much bigger speed. I think they're kind of running out of time. Millions of people getting out of these wagons. And it was night. And it was cold. And they told us men this way, women this way. Keep to each other, you know. I was standing with my mother, my brother, and me in one line, and another two people. Then come to face, which I know was Mengele, you know, a row of SS. And they were walking. And he with his stick, right, left, right, left, right, left. So that's when I was separated from my mother. And then I went-- went into Auschwitz itself. And we were straightaway stripped, but didn't know what was happening. We saw the chimneys. We smelled, we think. But didn't-- maybe my father or mother knew it. I didn't know what the chimneys were for, what was going on. But I kept on looking for my mother. You know, it was-- we'd never been away from her. I couldn't-- but you know, everything went so quick and so organized. You-- you couldn't look back or you couldn't-- you never thought of in five minutes you won't meet her. And we went in. We went in the shower. And they shaved our head. And they put the number on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show me the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind holding-- could you just-- [PAUSES 11 SECONDS] Were you given a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were given a uniform. And-- but they couldn't take us further, because there were so many transports arriving. So they said we should just wait, which usually didn't happen. Usually you had to carry on going wherever you were going to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sat. And one of the must have been a couple said to me, if you've got a number, you've nothing much to worry about, because that's working. So that was something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in all this panic, I don't know how, but the men got mixed up with the women, which never before happened or after that the transport got mixed up. And I-- I met my father. This was-- I think it was-- no. He was on his own. And he told me that he was standing with my brother and an uncle, an old man. So my brother was kind of put away with my uncle, went to the left, and my father went to the right. And he had a few words with me. We weren't allowed really strictly speaking to talk to each other or meet each other. And he spoke to me, and he said, now remember, you've got to survive. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [DRINKS]
